messages.content:
HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

Fix the hypothesis's targetNodes/perturbation so it passes validation WITHOUT changing the claim's meaning. If the claim cannot be expressed against funnel-active nodes, translate it into the funnel nodes it would actually move.

FUNNEL-ACTIVE NODES:
tamBase (EUR_M, value 300)
geo.DE (ratio, value 0.5)
geo.NL (ratio, value 0.28)
geo.PL (ratio, value 0.07)
geo.CH (ratio, value 0.08)
geo.CZ (ratio, value 0.02)
geo.AT (ratio, value 0.02)
geo.other (ratio, value 0.03)
seg.hyperscale (ratio, value 0.44)
seg.colocation (ratio, value 0.18)
seg.enterprise (ratio, value 0.29)
seg.edge (ratio, value 0.05)
seg.telecom (ratio, value 0.04)
cust.operator-large (ratio, value 0.4)
cust.operator-mid (ratio, value 0.25)
cust.oem (ratio, value 0.22)
cust.distributor (ratio, value 0.13)
serviceableFactor (ratio, value 0.55)
obtainableFactor (ratio, value 0.01)

HYPOTHESIS:
{
 "id": "colo-spec-contagion-from-hyperscale-tenants",
 "title": "Wholesale colocation inherits hyperscaler power specs: the 18% colo slice — modeled with zero sensitivity band — follows the substitute out of category",
 "narrative": "The model treats colocation as the PDU-native safe harbor: seg.colocation carries a null sensitivity band, so it never appears in the tornado at all. But CBRE shows European colo take-up being outstripped by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenant demand — and in wholesale/prelet halls the tenant, not the operator, dictates the rack power architecture. If hyperscale tenants specify busway plus power shelves inside leased halls (the same migration the exemplar tracks in self-build), the colo segment's PDU content leaves the category through the tenant spec sheet while the operator's capacity numbers keep growing 19% a year. The one segment the engine treats as certain is the one with the untracked contagion path.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.colocation"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "European colocation take-up is expected to outstrip new supply driven specifically by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenants — the demand filling CE colo halls is the same buyer cohort migrating to busway/power-shelf architectures, and in wholesale preleases that tenant writes the fit-out power spec",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c15",
    "digest:seg-mix#c13"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "As hyperscale tenants standardize substitute architectures inside leased halls, colo PDU content per MW falls even as colo capacity grows near double-digit and supply across the top-5 markets grows a record 20% — capacity metrics stay healthy while category-specific demand erodes",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c18",
    "digest:seg-mix#c16"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "seg.colocation 0.18 is single-source with a null band: the engine registers zero downside for this slice, so a tenant-spec-driven erosion of colo PDU demand is structurally invisible in every sensitivity output the client's team has reviewed",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.colocation",
    "digest:seg-mix#c1"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Colocation reads as the segment where rack PDUs are structurally mandatory (per-rack metering, multi-tenant billing), and the node's null band means it never surfaces in any sensitivity view — the team's eyes are drawn to banded nodes. The kill path runs through the wholesale/retail split inside the 18%, a distinction the model does not carry at all.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "Fit-out specifications from 2026 CE wholesale colocation preleases (Frankfurt, Amsterdam) showing rack PDUs — tenant-supplied or operator-supplied — as the standard rack power distribution in AI-density halls rather than busway/shelf architectures.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.3,
  "rationale": "Tenant spec authority in wholesale preleases is well documented and the buyer cohort is verifiably the migrating one, but retail colo and standard-density wholesale (the majority of current CE colo MW) remain PDU-native; the risk concentrates in the AI-density prelet growth tranche within the 12-month window",
  "basis": "judgment"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.colocation",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.12,
   "note": "Wholesale/prelet AI-density share of colo (roughly a third of the slice) migrates to tenant-specified busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack power architecture named in wholesale prelease fit-out specifications for AI-density halls",
   "where": "Frankfurt and Amsterdam wholesale colo prelease announcements and fit-out tender documents; DCD/DCNN build coverage",
   "threshold": "≥2 CE wholesale preleases specifying busway/power-shelf distribution as tenant standard",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Colo operator capex-call language on standardizing power distribution for AI halls",
   "where": "Digital Realty and Equinix EMEA quarterly capex calls and investor-day fit-out cost breakdowns",
   "threshold": "Explicit mention of busway or shelf standardization for new AI capacity",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE colo segmentation study splitting the 18% slice into retail vs wholesale/prelet MW and the power architecture specified in each",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.colocation",
   "note": "Replaces a null-band single-source node with a composition-aware estimate — the tornado currently cannot see this node at all"
  },
  {
   "action": "Anchor Year-1 sales in retail and managed colocation, where per-outlet metered PDUs are tied to tenant billing and remain architecturally sticky",
   "type": "strategic",
   "voiNodeId": null,
   "note": "Retail colo billing requirements make PDU displacement commercially costly for the operator"
  }
 ],
 "lens": "boundary-substitution",
 "riskId": "risk.boundary-substitution.colo-spec-contagion-from-hyperscale-tenants"
}

VALIDATION ERROR:
[
  {
    "code": "invalid_value",
    "values": [
      "error",
      "risk"
    ],
    "path": [
      0,
      "resolution"
    ],
    "message": "Invalid option: expected one of \"error\"|\"risk\""
  }
]

Emit the corrected hypothesis JSON (same shape).

If hyperscale tenants specify busway plus power shelves inside leased halls (the same migration the exemplar tracks in self-build), the colo segment's PDU content leaves the category through the tenant spec sheet while the operator's capacity numbers keep growing 19% a year.
